does: This paper audits the depth and robustness of biosecurity refusals in language models by testing whether refusals remain consistent under modest perturbations to prompt framing, formatting, and output length constraints. The authors evaluate five model architectures using 75 test prompts across benign and hazard-adjacent biology queries. They find that refusals are fragile: Gemma 2 2B-IT never genuinely refuses but hedges on hazard-adjacent queries; Gemma 4 E2B-IT's refusal rate drops from 65/75 to 0/75 when chat-template formatting is removed; both Gemma models collapse to zero refusal under 80-token output caps. The study reveals problematic patterns including over-refusal (Qwen and Phi-3 flagging 83-87% of benign biology), inconsistent refusal depth (Llama 3.2 showing a 61-point spread), and refusals that track legal status and cultural salience rather than actual CBRN hazard, demonstrated via Schedule I compounds like psilocybin cultivation.
connects: This work builds on growing efforts to evaluate language model safety and biosecurity alignment beyond binary hazard-detection measures. It extends prior work on prompt robustness and jailbreaking by examining whether safety behaviors withstand distribution shift, drawing on literature showing that model behaviors often collapse under minor input variations. The paper relates to sparse autoencoders (SAEs) for mechanistic interpretability, using both general and domain fine-tuned SAEs to probe what internal features drive refusal decisions. It connects to broader conversations about whether models learn robust safety principles versus surface-level pattern matching, and builds on emerging biosecurity evaluation frameworks that go beyond checking if models produce dangerous outputs to examining the principled consistency of their refusals.
enables: This audit points toward stronger biosecurity evaluation methodologies that test refusal robustness rather than assuming surface-level refusal indicates genuine safety alignment. It suggests future work on mechanistic understanding of refusal behavior through SAE analysis, potentially enabling interventions that make refusals more robust to prompt variation and better calibrated to actual hazard. The findings motivate development of biosecurity evaluations that distinguish between different failure modes—fragile refusals, over-refusal based on cultural signals, and over-refusal on benign queries. The work enables more targeted model improvements, such as ensuring safety behaviors persist across formatting changes and output constraints, and supports the development of evaluation frameworks that can identify whether models learn principled hazard discrimination versus spurious correlations to legality or sensitivity.
